Thank you so much, Julieta

author: @awfultruth6216
text:
After watching this video, 2 things happened.

1) I have learned something new.
2) I ordered the parts to build my own version of it. 

I decided to do a slightly different version of it, a simplified, hopefully working version: I'll use a 3/4" tee, a 3/4" to 1/2" reducer and a 1/2" pipe to make the "heart" of the burner. 3 parts, all threaded, no tools needed. 8.20 $ in materials, 2 mins of work. The chimney? A transparent, heat resisting material. This way it will double up as a light. thrn a connector to another pipe that leads to the exterior of the house to spare the ceiling and get CO2 out of room. Continuous feed option included.

author: @awfultruth6216
text:
At this point I'm curious what the distance can the oil climb on its own on the carbon felt.
It would be really nice to put the reservoir under the burner - just like in a moderator lamp. Air pipe that goes through the oil tank straight to the flame. Obviously' the whole structure would need some clearance to allow some air to enter the air pipe, but that would be an easy modification.
What do you think? Will the oil be too thick and heavy?

author: @perfoperfo9910
text:
Great videos Robert, I'm slowly trying to make my way through them but landed here due to your very nice series on simple heaters and burners.  I know you like a challenge so I hope you wont mind me explaining my dilemma..
I have a problem in that I have loads of used vegetable oil and a fairly decent work shop plus a cheap jacuzzi/hot tub and I want to try and find a away of combining those in a helpful manner🙂  My lazy spa hot tub has a 2Kw heater built in. It takes about 24 hours to reach temp then operates the heater when it needs to to top up but this can be very often.. at £0.42 a Kwh I haven't dared turn it on as that means possibly over £200 a month to run which is ridiculous.. any ideas how I could make a heater for it using waste vegetable oil ? Doesn't matter if it's 10Kw heater or whatever as I'd just divert the hot water to a radiator or something to control the temp. I'm quite handy with electronics and software so control isn't likely to be an issue..

author: @DMetcalfe92
text:
Hi Robert, 
Thankyou so much for everything you do. You're a fountain of knowledge and I'm a huge fan of you and your ideas!

I'm in the process of building this heater, I want to trial it in my greenhouse down at my allotment for winter. I just got the felt yesterday and I'm very excited! 

I've had an idea today, and think the carbon wick might be an excellent idea to use as a floating wick. I know you had some trouble pulling heavy oils up in another video, but floating it on the surface should solve that problem.

I've been doing a few experiments with sunflower oil at home. The wick burns beautifully sitting in a tray, like you show in another video, but I'm struggling to maintain a flame with a floating wick in a jar. It might be a lack of oxygen, or perhaps not enough thermal mass to maintain a flame. The floating wick is probably only 1/4 of the size of the one I had sitting in a tray. 

I'll drop you another comment if I manage any success! If you want to experiment, I'm more than happy for you to make a video on this idea, and get the community talking about it!

Thanks again! I appreciate everything you do and share for us!

author: @yougeo
text: Remember also the swirl principle which if you're trying to maximize the degree of a physical interaction happening in a small space you can effectively lengthen the small space in this case the chimney by forcing it all to go in a swirling pattern. So the bottom of your chimney should have vents that actually all open in a swirling pattern to induce a swirl in your chimney so more of that heat is kept lower in the chimney for longer and the gas is swirling in the chimney have more time to totally combust rather than just going straight up the chimney at high speed

author: @yougeo
text: The best oil boat heater uses two drip valves just for safety so you can always turn the oil off but instead of using a wick it uses a mass of coiled iron which is probably just a big piece of thick spring at the bottom of the drip pan that retains enough heat once it gets going to make the next strip of oil once again burn. So it doesn't use a wick at all. What that does mean is that you have to start it usually using some alcohol or having some sort of a flame that's constant that gets that massive iron at the bottom hot enough to sustain the flame. And of course in a boat an oil heater like those used in the Alaskan fishing boats or even sailboats sailing up in Norway and things they use a chimney to vent the exhaust gases outside of the boat and they try to wrap that chimney in a metal screening to both protect people from burning themselves against it but also to transfer more of the heat from the chimney to the screening and the surface area which releases more of the heat in the cabin of the boat before it's vented outside

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Simplicity is beautiful. But I've alwase been concerned with incomplete burning so it lead me down this thought path. 

One could figure a way to vent to the outside, but there's a challenge to collect as much heat as possible. And the more heat you extract the closer it gets to room temp with increasingly reduced convective flow. One gets to the point that they need forced air flow.  Pulling even more heat out, eventually the hydrogen from burned hydrocarbons makes water vapor and condenses out giving up its heat of vaporization.

But we dont have to stop here. Using a small heat pump, one could remove even more heat to where the final  exhaust gas temps are lower than room temp, taking a little electrical energy and getting 4-5 maybe 6 times as much heat.  The reason I sugest a likly high coefficient of performance is probable because the temp of the incoming exhaust gas is still above ambient room temp.

author: @icebluscorpion
text: Should I surprise you even more? Make that chimney pipe longer exactly so long that the heat source is exactly at 1/4 the length of the pipe. You will then get a thermoacustic effect. You will turn some of the heat energy into Acustic energy. And if you get it just right then it pumps so much Acustic energy in the system that it destroys itself XD. Except you convert some in electricity

replies.author: @ryanlebeck259
replies.text:
@nathandean1687 yes I do; this leads to a reduction and breakdown of some of the heavier lipids and allows for an easier separation during the treatment process to convert it into biodiesel.

Not all parts of the bacon grease are effective for the method and clarifying and reducing the bacon grease leads to a cleaner burn.

author: @brianmurphy8790
text:
Wicks soon get full of hard carbon crud when using heavy oils. 
Motor or veg oil wont work for long - even if you think you're clever by diluting it with kerosene. 
Clean thin oils (castor, olive, hemp, fish) will work sort of alright with wicks.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: I wonder if there's a good way to clean a wick that's been gummed up like that. People who vape might also be interested in that. I've found hydrogen peroxide to be somewhat effective in that regard. Still tastes funny, but it makes it nice and white again. Takes a good overnight soak with 3%.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe you are doing something wrong with your wicks mate - wicks are a centuries long technology and if you think they are out of date you haven't checked out how a commercial heater is made - I must get one and show folks - good idea thanks mate

replies.author: @brianmurphy8790
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering 
Didn't do anything wrong with the wicks, other than use veg oil that gums 'em up with glycerine, which then becomes crusty chunks of carbon.
The flame simply doesn't get hot enough to burn it away.
As for a commercial heater. I don't recall suggesting wicks were out of date tech.
Got one, a blue flame Valor Valmin (hollow wick) that I've just bout 50 litres of kerosene and 2 new wicks for.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
That should heat a decent size room,  the size of a single car garage anyway, if it is sealed from the elements at all,  but if it's insulated, it should heat it to below 0°F maybe -20 to -30°F fairly well, it should keep it above freezing anyway! But I'd about bet, it could heat it to 75-80° F on the coldest nights,  and use a oil lamp chimney can output a bright light!! That is a nice looking lamp/heater  to really step it up, you could build a series of them spaced about 6" from each other, on a piece of sheet steel  bent in the shape of a rectangle, or possibly a trough ,  to catch any possibility of spillage,  maybe attach it to a plywood box, to be off to one side, to hold the fuel supply, with the sheet steel blocking any heat,  with about 4burners, maybe 6burners, with a length of about 3' ( or 1,meter,)  with the sheet steel bent in a L shaped trough, with angle brackets reinforcing it,    with a 12" x 12" x 36" long wood body that would hold the fuel tank on top, and the burners off one side,  maybe add legs to it, 24" to 36"  to get it table high,  I also believe a similar design would work with propane!! 🔥 🔥 🔥 🔥 
Use a steel chimney, say 2" to 2.5"   with  with 6"x 6  ⬛ square pieces of sheet steel with a round hole in center,  with a 12" -18" chimney fit the sheets  fitting tightly, from about 3"- 4" high, about 1/4" -1/2" spacing, spot-weld each piece in about 6,places, per side if possible, or spot weld about every 1/8", to help with heat transfer,  and use about 8-10 pieces per chimney,  then a couple 4" computer style fans,  can be used, to move air across the fins, the fans placed about 12" away on the wood part,  to blow across the chimneys  maybe two chimneys per fan,  maybe use deflectors from thin aluminum sheet, to use a single 4"-6" fan to blow across them all,   cuta piece triangle shaped going from close to the length of the whole  thing about 36",  and  peak the fan size, but the wider side , spaced about 2" -3" apart, fit the sides with pieces to fill it in, use aluminum foil tape, to attach the pieces, leave  openings about 2" at each burner,  mid fins, about 4-6" from them, , this all can be attached to a piece of plywood, using a piece of chicken wire fence, or similar, to build a protective metal  covering,       And a fuel tank from a Chinese pocket bike, gas powered bicycle, or Chinese  minibike,  or a Chinese diesel caravan , camper heater fuel tank can be used, they hold around 2gallons,  and are thin and square, to stay away from the heat, but the small bike tanks hold about 1-2 litres, the diesel heaters are nice, but power is required, 12/24v, and 3-6 amps , this could be 0 volts 0smps, 0 watts,  I think the diesel vehicle heaters use near 100-150- 200 watts during start up, 
 , but use less after startup possibly in the 25-50 watts I'm thinking 100 watts or less to run the fuel pump and blower and glow plug,   this design can use no power, or 250ma at 12v for a little fan!
And the thing can use larger burners, be made from steel pipe, and welded up, making it cheaper to build,  and a
Small fan can be used to blow aut throu the burner, a tiny 2" or so na a laptop fan, funneled and channeled through the burner, but carefully, very carefully because , well because 🔥 FIRE!!

author: @travismoore7849
text: One idea was to have sever flower pots as a type of ceramic radiator that used a small candle to heat up a steel bolt that would get hot and the ceramic pots would regulate the heat for warming up your room a bit. The same thing could be done with a incandescent bulb of about 60 watts or so. Though I have to credit the candle heater web pages and youtube pages. And I tried something with an old steel pot and a ceramic water dish and some cheap little candles in the aluminum cans. It worked to help knock a bit of the chill out. But during minus 20 F weather when the power is off well it may be best to have a wood stove with soap stone around it. As it may not make enough heat in extreme cold weather. And I doubt you would leave an open flame all night long in your bedroom due to risk of fire.

author: @justinw1765
text:
Was looking at the smoke points of various different oils/fats, and noticed that there is a trend/pattern of unrefined oils having lower smoke temps than their refined counterparts. Makes me wonder what would be the most advantageous crop to grow at home to supply said oil? Obviously it will depend on the area and climate you live at, but I was thinking that sunflower might be one of the easier and more efficient ones in general? 

  On a different tangent, I was recently looking at rocket stove designs, and saw one made out of a wood post. The nice thing about it, was that it was light and insulating, but obviously it consumed itself over time. I immediately thought, why not protect the wood from burning, and have a nice, well insulated rocket stove that is super easy to make (literally just drilling two holes)?  One could coat the outside of the bored areas with water glass, and then do like a liner of ceramic fiber, NightHawkInLight's approx Starlite material, or the like.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: There's a kind of palm that's used extensively for oil production. They grow a ton of it in... Malaysia maybe? Somewhere around the South Pacific. Not sure how it does in other climates though. Otherwise, maybe peanuts?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: this is one of the reasons I don't go in for specific details mate and tend to try and give general guidance - so much varies with locality - efficiency is one aspect - availability is another - I am sure sunflower oil would be good - but palm oil is more readily available in lots of parts of the world - waste oils vary hugely in availability and usability - very often the only way to proceed is to try it and see I am afraid

author: @martinbonner8626
text: We had an oil heater explode when oil with water in it overheated , a mention of this could have been helpful

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: Was the tank vented? I've gotten water out of engine oil by just steaming it off before. The crankcase was vented into the air intake, so the steam just went through the engine and out the exhaust. Might be a good idea to put a vent tube from the top of the tank into the bottom of the chimney on a design like this.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: how the hell did you get it to explode? that's pretty difficult to do mate - I feel some kind of congratulations is in order - but on a serious note care is always needed when working with fuels and fire - but most folks know that and those that don't - well they will soon learn

replies.author: @martinbonner8626
replies.text: Rob , this was a regular oil heater , water was under the oil that was being heated . A B.L.E.V.C.E (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Cloud Explosion) situation arose ……. water and oils are not a good mix . In this case super heated water separated into hydrogen and oxygen …. wasn’t pretty in an enclosed shed

author: @551moley
text: Hi Rob, this is another possible addition to store heat in a sand battery, I've got an idea doing loops in my head! linking lots of potential heat sources, I've got excess electricity that I can't use for many months of the year, I've got a simple? way to put excess KW into a sand battery and a way to make use of it, both using readily available parts from old appliances. The bit I'm trying to work out is, A,  how much can say a 1000kg of sand hold in KW heating it between 100°C and 400°C and B, using std thermolite and rockwool insulation how much in KW's per day would you lose? Polar night use a vacuum tank and 100 tons of sand, I'm thinking "slightly" smaller with readily available insulation.

author: @marvinharms1891
text: Thank you Sir for all your uploading. I will never what my engineering science lecturer told us. "Energy cannot be created or destroyed, but only changes from one form to another." I've been fascinated with this ever since. I thank you Sir, and people like you that I'm not blind, dismembered....or dead! Because If I still had a library card...I could be🤕😉👍

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: I am not so certain about that quote. If it cant be destroyed nor created or depleted then what about entropy or the universal law of decay? Everything is in a constant state of decay like oxidation but you could argue that the evolution of species is a constant of interwoven expansion and the opposite of decay and Infact is increasing  just as is population ? For their to be equilibrium there must be a potential higher than the bottom and lower than the maximum state. So that would suggest lower than man made measurement of zero. Relative to what, would the zero be ? I think quotes like that can have their place to explain theories that meet their purpose say in trade schools to electrician that wire up houses or fix machines etc but in the wider world of electrical physics it is still argued to this day what electricity actually is and what properties it has . Its no different to Newton with what goes up ,must come down . It doesn't explain gravity but allows us to honour a force and to a degree work with it and appreciate it as a phenomenon . We are a long way from understanding these things with our current consciousness  and measurement devices but somehow maybe only around the corner at the same time . I think the masses find change hard to cope with as it is just as covid has shown so maybe we shouldnt be in any rush to change the status quo except with pollution of our planet ! Just a thought👍🏻

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - glad you are not mate - cheers

@Buzzhumma if you follow the electric universe hypnosis ,you realise there Are vast amounts of 'flowing electromagnetic energy' in interconnected inter galaxy webs that would destroy our little corner of the sol with just a blip.

hell even the -earth, wind, water, fire, gravity- mathamagic did it Flat Universe physicists world has more direct electric in it than earth & off world Could ever use.
gravity did it= never get really out there.
EU interconnected plasma web= we can make electromagnetic devices to tap & ride those for deep, deep space rovers, scopes & meshed micro Sat's to use as their base 'magnetic rivers & coms lines'

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: The law of conservation of energy is usually pretty reliable. The only place where it really breaks is on the supergalactic scale when you start talking about the expansion of the universe. Then you get to understand why conservation laws exist in the first place and when they can fail. (See Noether's theorem if you're interested - it's all about symmetries in nature.) As far as entropy, that's just a function of probabilities and large numbers. Self organizing systems like living organisms can reduce or maintain their local entropy by exporting the entropy to the external environment, but if you zoom out far enough then entropy is always increasing. That's one definition of the "arrow of time."

replies.author: @The.Word.1Way.2386
replies.text: @DFPercush can you see these laws?  what came first matter or universal laws, like gravity, momentum...? if matter came into existence with the big bang, surely the laws had to be in place

author: @Buzzhumma
text: Hi Rob , many moons ago I wrapped a copper coil around a rocket stove flu and dripped oil into the combustion area. As it creates more heat it would draw more air and so on and so on. I had an inline valve to regulate the flow. It seemed to work perfect and regulated its oxygen needed automatically as it would just burn any vapour in the flu and create more heat and just draw more air . I think it still needed a supply of wood at least at the start the way I had it  but I never got to finish experimenting with that but always wondered if it could sustain without wood in the combustion area.I guess this concept you show here proves it would be able if there is conductive air flow! 👍🏻
